rich_text.plain_text: "Student Performance" AND "Semi-Supervised Learning"

text.content: Twenty First Pacific Asia Conference on Information Systems

rich_text.plain_text: Twenty First Pacific Asia Conference on Information Systems
Google Scholar
Intervention & Population
Nezami et al.
Abstract

blocks.text: Using computer vision to detect student engagement is a daunting task due to multiple domain specificities. Moreover, the number of available datasets is also lacking. To address the issue, the authors propose a semi-supervised learning framework on data that only has a small portion of annotated instances. Results show that the proposed methodology S4VM outperforms the purely supervised method and achieves acceptable results on the literature.

text.content: Using computer vision to detect student engagement is a daunting task due to multiple domain specificities. Moreover, the number of available datasets is also lacking. To address the issue, the authors propose a semi-supervised learning framework on data that only has a small portion of annotated instances. Results show that the proposed methodology S4VM outperforms the purely supervised method and achieves acceptable results on the literature.

Introduction

blocks.text: User engagement is regarded as an important proxy for quality of teaching and learning. User Engagement measures come in 3 types:

text.content: User engagement is regarded as an important proxy for quality of teaching and learning. User Engagement measures come in 3 types:

rich_text.plain_text: User engagement is regarded as an important proxy for quality of teaching and learning. User Engagement measures come in 3 types:
Self-Reports (subjective)
Observational checklists and rating scales (subjective)
Automated measures (objective)

blocks.text: Automated measures have been gaining attention due to rise of cameras. The paper uses facial expression videos to detect user engagement in a computer-supported educational environment.

text.content: Automated measures have been gaining attention due to rise of cameras. The paper uses facial expression videos to detect user engagement in a computer-supported educational environment.

rich_text.plain_text: Automated measures have been gaining attention due to rise of cameras. The paper uses facial expression videos to detect user engagement in a computer-supported educational environment.

blocks.text: Data requires an extensive amount of annotation, thus the question becomes whether it is possible (via semi-supervised learning) to detect User Engagement with few amounts of annotated data. To explore this question, the authors use S4VM, a semi-supervised approach to support vector machines claimed to work at the same level of the vanilla algorithm.

text.content: Data requires an extensive amount of annotation, thus the question becomes whether it is possible (via semi-supervised learning) to detect User Engagement with few amounts of annotated data. To explore this question, the authors use S4VM, a semi-supervised approach to support vector machines claimed to work at the same level of the vanilla algorithm.

rich_text.plain_text: Data requires an extensive amount of annotation, thus the question becomes whether it is possible (via semi-supervised learning) to detect User Engagement with few amounts of annotated data. To explore this question, the authors use S4VM, a semi-supervised approach to support vector machines claimed to work at the same level of the vanilla algorithm.
Related work

blocks.text:
Engagement is especially useful metric to follow because engaged students pay more attention and are more likely to explore (even if they make mistakes). The lietrature features a small amount of works measuring engagement from facial features.
Moreover, works that have used this approach often rely on the combination of video and self-reports to make the annotation of the video. In addition, models to predict engagement in the literature make an overall analysis at the level of the session, but provide little insights over the state of the engagement of the student on a moment by moment basis.

text.content:

blocks.text: Another work by Bosch was able to be used, but achieved AUC scores not greater than 0.68. Additional models intended to measure engagement also added other variables to the facial recognition such as heart rate. Current state of the art is Whitehill et al. or Monkaresi et al.

Methods

blocks.text: The data includes students attending 9th or 10th grade. Data involved the collection of fourteen 30 minutes sessions (20 fps). The dataset is not fully annotated, suggesting an SSL approach. The approach is summarized as follows:

text.content: The data includes students attending 9th or 10th grade. Data involved the collection of fourteen 30 minutes sessions (20 fps). The dataset is not fully annotated, suggesting an SSL approach. The approach is summarized as follows:

blocks.text: The data contains video footage in reading and writing tasks. A selection of 6 videos and 1000 frames considered reliable was done to annotate. Sample included 563 engaged frames and 437 non-engaged frames. Engagement is identified via immersion and  concentration on the system (but not frustration). Annontation was eprformed by multiple annotators and had 0.78 of agreement between anotators.

Feature extraction and Selection

blocks.text: Authors use Facial Action Units, specifically through the OpenFace Toolkit and decrease dimensionality using PCA (unspecified number of components kept, only that 95% of the variability is kept.

blocks.text: Model used is S4VM, a safe version for semi-supervised learning that does not underperform supervised models such as SVM.
